twitter.hook: Denmark banned ashwagandha from food supplements in 2023. Iceland's regulatory system flagged cholestatic liver injury cases in 2017-2018. NIH LiverTox lists it as a known hepatotoxic herb. This information is not in the supplement aisle.
thread: The cortisol finding is real: Chandrasekhar et al. (Indian J Psych Med, 2012) — 60-day RCT, n=64, KSM-66 root extract 300mg twice daily. Serum cortisol dropped 27.9% vs. placebo (p=0.0006). Perceived Stress Scale dropped 44% vs. 5.5% placebo. That result exists.

The form question matters more than the marketing admits. The '24 clinical trials' brands cite are almost entirely on KSM-66 — a specific root-only extract from Ixoreal Biomed, standardized to ≥5% withanolides. Sensoril and Shoden have separate, smaller trial bases. Generic pharmacy bottles have none of these trials.

The sleep finding: Langade et al. (Cureus, 2019) — 10-week RCT, n=150, Shoden extract. Sleep onset latency: 29.0 min vs. 33.9 min placebo (p < 0.019). Sleep efficiency: 83.5% vs. 79.7% (p < 0.001). Real finding, specific extract, specific population.

The testosterone finding that doesn't generalize: Lopresti et al. (2019) — 16-week crossover RCT, n=57, Shoden. Testosterone increased 14.7% more than placebo (p=0.010). Required population: overweight, aging men (40-70), with mild fatigue symptoms. Not young, lean, non-fatigued men.

What doesn't replicate: cognitive enhancement. The Alzheimer's Drug Discovery Foundation rates ashwagandha 'limited' for cognitive benefit — one tier below 'promising.' The evidence is from small trials in narrow populations without independent replication.

The hepatotoxicity file: Björnsson et al. (Liver International, 2020) — 5-case series, 3 from Iceland, 2 from U.S. Drug-Induced Liver Injury Network. Mean age 43 years. All developed jaundice 2-12 weeks after starting ashwagandha. Cholestatic injury. One biopsy: acute cholestatic hepatitis. No liver failures, but prolonged recovery.

The quality crisis: ConsumerLab testing found only 38% of ashwagandha products pass for quality, accurate labeling, and contamination limits. The trials showing effects used specific standardized extracts. The 62% that fail testing are not those extracts.

The Denmark ban (April 2023): Danish Veterinary and Food Administration cited a 2020 risk assessment from Technical University of Denmark flagging hormonal disruption concerns at physiological doses. The reproductive-risk basis has been disputed. The liver injury documentation predates and is independent of the ban.

Honest summary: KSM-66, 300-600mg, 8-12 weeks produces real, measurable cortisol reduction in stressed adults. Shoden produces real sleep improvement. Both have industry-funded trial bases. Hepatotoxicity is a documented adverse event. Most products on shelves are not the tested extracts.
twitter.cta: The cortisol finding is real. The liver risk is real. The 62% product failure rate is real: https://www.blackswanp.com/research/mental-work/ashwagandha-evidence — Share if someone in your life is taking the pharmacy version expecting KSM-66 results.

linkedin.hook:
Ashwagandha generates 1 million monthly searches in English. The wellness industry has built a $1.5 billion global market on it.

In 2023, Denmark became the first European nation to ban it from food supplements.

In 2020, Iceland and the U.S. Drug-Induced Liver Injury Network jointly published a case series on cholestatic liver injury.

The supplement aisle doesn't mention either of these things.
linkedin.body:
Ashwagandha has the densest evidence base of any adaptogen — and the most underreported risk profile.

What the high-quality trials actually show:

Cortisol: Chandrasekhar et al. (Indian J Psych Med, 2012) — 60-day RCT, n=64, KSM-66 extract. Serum cortisol dropped 27.9% vs. placebo (p=0.0006). The finding replicates across 7+ independent RCTs for KSM-66 specifically.

Sleep: Langade et al. (Cureus, 2019) — 10-week RCT, n=150, Shoden extract. Sleep onset latency reduced, sleep efficiency improved, both statistically significant versus placebo.

Testosterone: Lopresti et al. (American J Men's Health, 2019) — 16-week crossover, n=57. 14.7% greater testosterone increase vs. placebo — in overweight men, ages 40-70, with mild fatigue symptoms. That specific population. Not generalized.

The form caveat that most coverage misses: the 'clinical trials' brands cite are almost entirely on KSM-66 (Ixoreal Biomed), Sensoril (Natreon), or Shoden (Arjuna Natural) — proprietary standardized extracts, funded primarily by their manufacturers. Generic root powder has a different alkaloid profile and none of these trials.

ConsumerLab independent testing found only 38% of ashwagandha products pass for quality and label accuracy. The trials are on the 38%. Most products aren't.

The safety file:

Björnsson et al. (Liver International, 2020): 5 cases of ashwagandha-induced liver injury — 3 from Iceland, 2 from U.S. DILIN. Mean age 43. All developed jaundice 2-12 weeks post-initiation. Cholestatic or mixed pattern. NIH LiverTox lists ashwagandha as a known hepatotoxic herb.

Denmark banned ashwagandha from food supplements in April 2023 — the first EU country to do so.

Low absolute incidence does not mean low absolute risk when 1 million monthly searches are translating to product purchases.

Full citation-backed breakdown: https://www.blackswanp.com/research/mental-work/ashwagandha-evidence

slides.title: SLIDE 1 — HOOK
slides.body: Ashwagandha: $1.5 billion market. 1 million monthly searches. Denmark banned it in 2023. Iceland documented liver injuries in 2017. The supplement aisle mentions neither.

slides.title: SLIDE 2 — STAT
slides.body: Chandrasekhar et al., Indian J Psych Med 2012 (n=64, KSM-66 extract, 60 days): serum cortisol dropped 27.9% vs. placebo (p=0.0006). Perceived Stress Scale dropped 44% vs. 5.5% placebo. Real finding. Specific extract.

slides.title: SLIDE 3 — REVEAL
slides.body: ConsumerLab tested ashwagandha products: only 38% passed for quality, label accuracy, and contamination. The trials showing effects used specific standardized extracts. Most products on shelves are not those extracts.

slides.title: SLIDE 4 — DETAIL
slides.body: Björnsson et al., Liver International 2020: 5 cases of ashwagandha-induced cholestatic liver injury — Iceland + U.S. Drug-Induced Liver Injury Network. Onset: 2-12 weeks. NIH LiverTox lists ashwagandha as a known hepatotoxic herb.

slides.title: SLIDE 5 — QUOTE
slides.body: "Ashwagandha has been implicated in rare instances of clinically apparent liver injury, which is typically cholestatic or mixed in pattern." — NIH LiverTox database, 2023

slides.title: SLIDE 6 — IMPLICATION
slides.body: The testosterone finding needs a specific population: overweight men, ages 40-70, mildly fatigued, Shoden extract, 16 weeks. It does not generalize to the average supplement buyer.

slides.title: SLIDE 7 — SOURCES
slides.body: Chandrasekhar et al., Indian J Psych Med (2012) · Björnsson et al., Liver International (2020) · Langade et al., Cureus (2019) · Lopresti et al., AJMH (2019) · NIH LiverTox (2023)

slides.title: SLIDE 8 — CTA
slides.body: Read the full evidence file at blackswanp.com/research/mental-work/ashwagandha-evidence

reddit.title: Ashwagandha evidence breakdown: what actually replicates (cortisol yes, cognition no), the form problem, and the hepatotoxicity data nobody talks about
reddit.body:
I've been going through the primary ashwagandha literature carefully. Here's what I found when I read the papers rather than the supplement reviews.

WHAT ACTUALLY REPLICATES:

Cortisol/stress: Chandrasekhar 2012 (KSM-66, n=64, 60 days) — 27.9% cortisol reduction vs. placebo (p=0.0006), PSS dropped 44% vs. 5.5%. This replicates across 7+ RCTs for KSM-66. The anxiolytic effect is real for this extract at these doses.

Sleep: Langade 2019 (Shoden, n=150, 10 weeks) — sleep onset latency 29.0 vs. 33.9 min placebo (p < 0.019), sleep efficiency 83.5% vs. 79.7% (p < 0.001). Real finding.

WHAT DOESN'T REPLICATE:

Cognition: Alzheimer's Drug Discovery Foundation rates it 'limited' — one tier below 'promising.' The trials are small, narrow, and in populations far removed from most nootropics users.

THE FORM PROBLEM:

The '24 clinical trials' brands cite are almost entirely on KSM-66 (≥5% withanolides, root only), Sensoril, or Shoden — specific proprietary extracts. Generic ashwagandha root powder has a different alkaloid profile. A study showing effect on KSM-66 tells you nothing about a generic bottle.

ConsumerLab: only 38% of ashwagandha products pass quality testing.

THE HEPATOTOXICITY NOBODY MENTIONS:

Björnsson et al. (Liver International, 2020): 5 cases from Iceland + U.S. DILIN. Mean age 43. All jaundiced 2-12 weeks post-initiation. Cholestatic pattern. One biopsy: acute cholestatic hepatitis. NIH LiverTox lists ashwagandha as a known hepatotoxic herb.

Denmark banned it from food supplements in April 2023.

Conclusion: KSM-66 for cortisol is the most defensible use case. Stop and see a doctor if you develop dark urine or jaundice after starting. Don't use generic powder expecting KSM-66 results.

Full breakdown: https://www.blackswanp.com/research/mental-work/ashwagandha-evidence

reddit.title: The ashwagandha liver injury data: 5-case series from Iceland + US DILIN, what NIH LiverTox says, and why Denmark banned it
reddit.body:
Posting specifically about the safety side because it's almost never discussed in ashwagandha threads.

In 2020, Björnsson, Björnsson, Avula, et al. published a 5-case series in Liver International — a peer-reviewed gastroenterology journal. Three cases from Iceland (flagged by their regulatory system in 2017-2018), two from the U.S. Drug-Induced Liver Injury Network (DILIN).

Patient profile: mean age 43 years (range 21-62). All five developed jaundice 2-12 weeks after starting ashwagandha supplementation. Liver injury was cholestatic or mixed pattern in all cases. One biopsy showed acute cholestatic hepatitis. Pruritus and hyperbilirubinemia persisted 5-20 weeks after stopping. No patients developed liver failure. Liver tests normalized in 1-5 months.

NIH LiverTox (National Library of Medicine database tracking drug-induced liver injury): lists ashwagandha as a 'known hepatotoxic herb' with documented cases of clinically apparent liver injury, predominantly cholestatic.

Denmark: April 2023, Danish Veterinary and Food Administration became the first EU nation to ban ashwagandha from food supplements. Official reason: risk assessment from Technical University of Denmark flagging hormonal disruption concerns. The reproductive-risk basis has been disputed by independent researchers. The liver injury data is a separate, older, and harder-to-dismiss body of evidence.

Incidence is low relative to total users. But 'low incidence' applied to 1 million monthly searches means potentially thousands of cases annually where people don't connect their unexplained jaundice to the 'stress supplement' they started a month earlier.

Hard rule: stop immediately and see a doctor if you develop fatigue, dark urine, yellowing of skin or eyes, or upper-right abdominal discomfort while using ashwagandha.

Full evidence breakdown (including what does work): https://www.blackswanp.com/research/mental-work/ashwagandha-evidence

email.subject: The liver injury case series your supplement brand won't cite
email.preheader: Iceland flagged it in 2017. Denmark banned it in 2023. NIH LiverTox listed it. The cortisol signal is also real.
email.body:
In 2020, Björnsson et al. published a 5-case series in Liver International documenting ashwagandha-induced cholestatic liver injury — three cases from Iceland, two from the U.S. Drug-Induced Liver Injury Network. Onset: 2-12 weeks after starting. NIH LiverTox now lists ashwagandha as a known hepatotoxic herb.

In April 2023, Denmark banned ashwagandha from food supplements — the first European nation to do so.

Only 38% of ashwagandha products pass independent ConsumerLab quality testing.

The cortisol signal is real: Chandrasekhar et al. 2012 (KSM-66, n=64, 60 days) — 27.9% serum cortisol reduction vs. placebo. The sleep finding is real: Langade et al. 2019 (Shoden, n=150, 10 weeks). These results apply to specific standardized extracts at specific doses.

Both things are true. The signal is real and narrow. The risks are real and underreported.

Full evidence file: https://www.blackswanp.com/research/mental-work/ashwagandha-evidence

pullQuotes: Denmark banned it in 2023. NIH LiverTox lists it as hepatotoxic. This is not in the aisle.
Only 38% of ashwagandha products pass independent quality testing.
The testosterone finding requires overweight men, ages 40-70, mildly fatigued. Not you.
27.9% cortisol reduction in 60 days — for KSM-66, not for generic root powder.
The liver injury signal: onset 2-12 weeks. 5 documented cases. Cholestatic pattern.
